title: Cardiac Tamponade - Penetrating Trauma
chief_complaint: Gunshot wound to chest. Gasping for air. Losing consciousness.

physical_exam.Chest: GSW entry: Left parasternal 4th intercostal space. Exit: Left posterior axillary line 7th ICS.
physical_exam.Cardiac: BECK TRIAD: Hypotension + Muffled heart sounds + Distended neck veins (JVD). Pulsus paradoxus >15mmHg.
physical_exam.Lungs: Equal breath sounds bilaterally (differentiates from tension PTX).
physical_exam.eFAST: Large pericardial fluid collection (blood). Right ventricular diastolic collapse. Dilated IVC.

correct_diagnosis: Cardiac Tamponade from Penetrating Cardiac Injury - EMERGENCY RESUSCITATIVE THORACOTOMY
key_learning_points: Cardiac tamponade = accumulation of fluid/blood in pericardial space → compression of heart → decreased diastolic filling → decreased cardiac output → obstructive shock

Beck triad (present in only 30%): Hypotension + Muffled heart sounds + JVD. Pulsus paradoxus >10mmHg (decrease in SBP >10 during inspiration). Kussmaul sign (paradoxical JVP rise with inspiration) - constrictive pericarditis, NOT tamponade

eFAST: Pericardial fluid, RV diastolic collapse (specific), IVC plethora. CT if stable. Pericardiocentesis: Temporizing measure for non-traumatic tamponade. Aspiration of 10-50mL can dramatically improve hemodynamics

Traumatic tamponade: EMERGENCY RESUSCITATIVE THORACOTOMY (ED thoracotomy). Indications: Penetrating chest trauma + signs of life (<10 min from loss of vital signs). Left anterolateral thoracotomy 4th-5th ICS. Open pericardium vertically (avoid phrenic nerve). Evacuate clot. Control cardiac injury (Foley catheter balloon tamponade, finger occlusion, Satinsky clamp, staple or suture repair). Internal cardiac massage. Cross-clamp aorta for refractory shock
Non-traumatic tamponade: Pericardiocentesis (subxiphoid approach, aim toward left shoulder at 15°). Pericardial window if recurrent (malignant effusion). Treat underlying cause
Prognosis: Penetrating cardiac trauma survival: Stab wounds 30-50%, GSW 10-15% if signs of life on arrival. If require ED thoracotomy for blunt trauma: <2% survival
